content: We can start acceptance testing as soon as any of the parts of the project is complete. It demonstrates system conformance to - customer requiments - operationel processes - maintainance processes Usually based on the requirement specifications Independent from other types of testing mostly executed by the end users (and/or PO) Acceptance tests ensures that the system satisfies the customer's needs. - tests against the requirements specification Should be performed with the customer's involvement operational users beta customers comparny executives Commen in contractual situations successful tests completion leds the customer to accept the system Unsuccesfull test completion cen entitle the customer to reject the product(based on the written contract) SLA ( service level Agrement) Validation at the stage the product is not expected to fail the objective is not to find defects but to ensure that everything works according to specifications successfull acceptance tests can give the green light to product deployment. Acceptance tests clarify requirements comment ground between customers and developers in Agile, based on user stories they can procide a measure of "doneness" Must be comprehensive the whole system is tested In agile and iterative SDLC's(software development life cicle) it involces testeing all functionalities for each iteration do not change even if product implementation does. Types of Acceptance tests: • User Acceptance Testing (UAT) • Operational Acceptance Testing • Validation of the products’ operations in a real situation • Contract and Regulations Testing • Legal bonds, safety standards (banking, pharmaceutical), governmental regulations (defense) • Alpha Testing. In-house • Beta Testing. At the customer’s premises • Pilot phase. Product release on a limited geographical or logical scale UAT - User acceptance tests a test from the perspective of the users and other stateholders not just a tests of the software, nor of the functionality, performance, reliability and security nor of the usability of the system the nearest we get to "the real thing" without actually taking the risk of releasing the system into service.

part of integration testing its importen because between 60 and 80% of all internet traffic is API calls subcutaneous testing (under the skin testing, explain further) potential API errors: version changes endpoint name or paramenters changes. permissions changes(user that can only read, should not be able to write) server issues Network issues elements to test: Data format validation, to prevent fraudulent client requests. Authentocation, usually with a token permissions, since there might be more than one authorisation level Proper use of HTTP status codes besides 200 OK and 404 Not Found! 201 created 400 bad request (malformed request) 401 Unauthorized (well formed request, but the client does ot have permission) 500 internal server error (the api has a bug) 204 correct deletion further tests: how do a GET request behave when they return no data POST, PUT and PATCH requests: extremely long text values Unsanitised strings whether put requests should require all values to be filled how do delete requests behave

Setting up and tearing down unit tests: Setup and tear down set up - the "contructor of the testing class - fill out more here tear down - closes all open connections - fill out more here Test doubles: generic term for any case where you replace replace a production object for testing purposes. its fake objects that subsitute other objects in the codebase They allow yo: Test a unit in total isolation Substitute components not yet developed Speed up test execution Types of test doubles: mock: sending something to a database stub: Emulating incoming call. (data coming from an end point or database) Unit Testing: best practices: Each unit test must verify one and only one behaviour. avoid chaining arrange, act and assert sections unit tests may easyly become an integration test Avoid branching (no if,switch,while or for) Cover the business logic, and not much else. (only write unit tests if there is business logic) Code is not an assert but a liability more code -> higher risk of bugs if code architecture changes, tests may also change. Act section must only be one line of code otherwise there may be errors in code design if the test fails with a big act, its hard to know what went wrong. use a comprehensive approach to test case design perform black-box test desing look for numering boundaries and thier surroundings Do positive and negative testing complete the suite with tests chases All unit tests should be run upon every code change, no matter how small -> regression testing Good code design and structure is of the essense tight couplign prevent writing unit tests write pure methods as often as posible (return the same output based on the same input) Unit Testing anti-patterns never test private methods directly testing is then based in observable behaviour tests become coupled ti implementation tests are no longer resistant to refactoring if you need to tests a private method, use the white box technique and your knowledge of the code to give the tests the parameters its needs to reach said private method. Do never expose private state try to write a tests for the what the user would see. Do not leak domain knowlodfe to the unit test Always use hardcoded values or mock! avoid code polution! - changing your code for tests Private vs Shared dependensices private dependencies occur between code units. shared dependencies are those that are shared between tests tend to slow down the execution provide means for tests to effect each others outcome fx databases if one deletes a row and anthoer tests searches for it, the execution of the first test makes the second test fail. Unit tests involving shared dependencies might be considered integration tests. for the exam, be able to say what a unit test and integration test is for you. Volatile dependencies Introduce a requirement to configure a runtime environment a database an API service contain nondeterministic behaviour a random numbergenerator very difficult to test a

shared dependencies might be considered integration tests. for the exam, be able to say what a unit test and integration test is for you. Volatile dependencies Introduce a requirement to configure a runtime environment a database an API service contain nondeterministic behaviour a random numbergenerator very difficult to test a database is both a shared and a volitile dependency The classical Approach: a single unit of behaviour (Detroit approach) use tests doubles only to replace shared dependencies. "sociable" unit tests: collaborators are not mocked isolation of the unit tests, not tof the code under test The London approach ( the mockist approach) a single unit of code recommends to replace all dependencies by test doubles except for immutable dependencies(constants , enums) solidary unit tests: collaborators are mocked. total isolation of the code under test. benefits: small, if a error is found you know precisly where the error is.

code is written once, but read alot of times - write code to be read Type: Black box - specification - based - (sometimes low experience with code base) often written based on the specifications black - box techniques: 1\. equivalence Partitioning 2\. Boundary value analysis 3\. decision table testing 4\. State transition testing **1 Equivalence Partitioning:**   Input Partions:   similar inputs can be grouped together (positive integers, alphabetic characters)   any partitions calue can used for testing (often a middle value)   numbers: zero is a special case that consitute a partition of its own   input partions (based on input values)   example: program that accepts integers between -10000 and 10000   should be split in valid and non valid partitions:   valid:   integer between -10000 and -1 (inclusive)   for 0   integer between 1 and 10000 (inclusive)   non-valid:   below -10000   above 10000   non-integer numbers (edge chase)   non-numeric characters(edge chase)   this would translate into 3 "positive" tests(partitions) and 4 "negative" tests (partitions) Always remember edge chases!   Output Partitions:   These are based on outputs!   Depending on the input, we expect different Outputs. Kinda the same as input partions, find the inputs that give the expected outputs.     **In class exercise:** Bank account   Suppose you have a bank account that offers variable interest rates:   0.5 per cent for the first 10000 kr credit   1 per cent for the next 10000 kr   1.5 per cent for the rest   If you wanted to check that the bank was handling your account correctly:   What input partitions might you use?     Valid:   1. 0 > 10000.000 2. 10000.000 > 20000.000 3. 200000.000 < MAX   none-Valid:   1. 0 2. MIN < 0 if it can recieve inputs: 3. non-integer numbers 4. non-numeric characters       What test case values could be inferred from said partitions? **2. Boundary Value Analysis**   Partitions have boundaries   errors tend to cluster around boundaries   mostly bases on greater or lower than and loops   works well in combination with equivalence partitioning     Use the 3 boundary values approach - find the bounderies and test around them. ex 1 -99 test around 0 , 1 , 2 and 98,99,100     Error testing: try to have the application raise exceptions and errors - if you are using your own errors you need to write tests to accomidate those.   Parameter testing: Try edge chases in method/function parameters. **In class exercise:**     A wholesaler sells printer cartridges. The minimum order quantity is 5. There is a 20% discount for orders of 100 or more printer cartridges. You have been asked to prepare test cases using various values for the number of printer cartridges ordered.   Use black-box analysis to identify a comprehensive series of test cases:   Identify the corresponding equivalence partitions and propose test cases based on them     valid: 1. >=

printer cartridges. You have been asked to prepare test cases using various values for the number of printer cartridges ordered.   Use black-box analysis to identify a comprehensive series of test cases:   Identify the corresponding equivalence partitions and propose test cases based on them     valid: 1. >= 5 2. >=100 invalid: 3. <5     Use 3-value boundary value analysis to identify further test cases     test chase: 4,5,6 99,100,101 3,4,5 -> 3,4,5,6,99,100,101     Write down the full resulting list of test cases   Implement the discount calculation function in code and write the corresponding unit tests in the language and unit test framework of your choice **In class Exercise:**   A password field accepts a minimum of 6 characters and a maximum of 10 characters. Define:   Its corresponding equivalence partitions and test case values:   valid: 1. 5 >= 10 8 5,6,7 9,10,11   non-valid:   2. 1 >= 5 2 0,1,2 4,5,6 ' 3. 10 < 542 10,11,12   4. 0 0 0     The boundary values and resulting test case values with a 3-boundary value approach   The final list of test case values     0,1,2,4,5,6,7,8,9,10,11,12,542 **In Class Exercise:** You are testing the payment functionality of an e-shop. The system receives a positive amount of purchases in kroner with an accuracy of 1 øre. Based on this value, a discount is calculated according to the following rules:   Amount Discount   Up to 300 kr 0%   Over 300 kr, up to 800 kr 5%   Over 800 kr 10%   Use black-box analysis to identify a comprehensive series of test cases:   Identify the corresponding equivalence partitions and propose test cases based on them   Use 3-value boundary value analysis to identify further test cases   valid:   1. 1 <=300 50 0% 0,1,2 299,300,301   2. 300 <= 800 500 5% 300,301,302 799,800,801   3. 800 < 5000 10% 800,801,802   non-valid:   4. 0 0 error 0   5. < 0 -50 error -2,-1,0     Write down the full resulting list of test cases:   -50,-2,-1,0,1,2,50,299,300,301,302,500,799,800,801,802,5000     Implement the discount calculation function in code and write the corresponding unit tests in the language and unit test framework of your choice White box - the developper knows the code and how to test it

In class exercise: Bank account Suppose you have a bank account that offers variable interest rates: 0.5 per cent for the first 10000 kr credit 1 per cent for the next 10000 kr 1.5 per cent for the rest If you wanted to check that the bank was handling your account correctly: What input partitions might you use? **Hard you can kick, software you can only curse at.** ***Introduction:*** Why do we test - because software fails. **Why does software fail?:** Developers makes mistakes Errors in sprecifications, design, code: bad user Stories. presure: deadline, complexity of systems, change of technologies **What does testing do?** helps examining the code finding errors preventing defects / preventing errors from happening **When is a test good?** When it finds errors! / breaks the system Dont write millions of test, but good tests. **How much should we test?** Depends on how much time and money we have ***Terminology:*** **Debugging vs Testing:** **Debugging:** The developers indentify the cause of bugs or defects in code and correct them. Correct / fix code. **Testing:** Find bugs / you dont always fix the bugs. **Static testing.** the code is not executed (linting / sonarcube) **Dynamic testing.** Execution of code with test data. **Positive vs Negative testing:** **Happy path vs unhappy path.** **Positive (happy).** The code is supposed to execute correctly. **Negative tested (unhappy).** The code is supposed to fail / try to break the code. ***Testing Concepts:*** **Regression testing:** Does the new code, effect the rest of the system? if it does, a regression has been found. Test basis The body of knowledge used as the basis for test analysis and design •Quality Management= Quality Assurance + Quality Control •Quality Assurance (QA). Proactive.Making sure that processes are undertaken correctly •Quality Control (QC). Reactive.Making sure that the desired level of quality is achieved •Testing. Enforcement of quality control by finding defects in a product **Verification vs Validation:** Work product - Intermediate of deliverable of the final product **Verification:** writing tests to verify the acceptance criteries are covered ' **Validation:** The PO approving the product based the acceptance criteries based on the tests (verification). **General Testing Principles (we will be asked about 4 of them at the exam)** 1. **When we test we can prove bugs exist, but we can never prove they dont exist.** **2. The main purpose of testing is to find as many defects as possible.** **3. Defect clustering:** -Defects are not distributed uniformly either in the software or over time. -The Pareto 80-20 rule: 20% of the code can have 80% of the errors. **4. Tests ear out ( = pesticide paradox)** - tests lose their ability to detect errors over time. - Tests must be regularly reviewed and modified. - with developers experience increasing, reduced testing might be fine. **5. Testing is context dependent** -Risk is a key aspect -You have to think about what you test and how. **6. Absence of errors fallacy** - Never assume that a product is error free - Even verified systems might not pass validation **7. Exhaustive testing is impossible** **8. Early testing is of the essence:** Reasons: -Many errors have to do with specifications -It is cheaper Exhaustive Testing is Impossible!
is impossible** **8. Early testing is of the essence:** Reasons: -Many errors have to do with specifications -It is cheaper Exhaustive Testing is Impossible!

The 4 T's : Traits - Task - Tone - Targets   **Trait:** How you want the LLM to think when its answering questions,   You could give it roles or specify a specifig behavior     **Task:** Be as specific as possible   **Tone:** Style of language that the LLM should use   - clear language   - straigtforward language   - stuff to avoid (fx hype language)   - use a formal or proffesionel language   **Target:** All these T's are giving the LLM a context for who the answer is for and how it should construct said answer.

# LLM for Developers - Course Notes ## Introduction to Large Language Models Large Language Models (LLMs) are deep learning models trained on massive text datasets to understand and generate human-like text. They use transformer architectures and attention mechanisms to process language contextually. ### Key Concepts: - **Transformer Architecture**: Uses self-attention to weigh importance of different words in a sentence. - **Pre-training**: Models learn general language patterns by predicting masked words or next tokens. - **Fine-tuning**: Adapting pre-trained models to specific tasks with smaller labeled datasets. ## Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) RAG enhances LLMs by connecting them with external knowledge sources. Instead of relying solely on internal parameters, RAG retrieves relevant documents before generating responses. ### How RAG Works: 1. **Retrieve**: Search an index for relevant documents based on the input query. 2. **Augment**: Add retrieved documents as context to the original prompt. 3. **Generate**: Use the augmented prompt to produce a more informed response. This approach allows models to provide up-to-date and source-grounded answers while reducing hallucinations. ## Prompt Engineering Basics Effective prompting guides LLM behavior without retraining. Structured templates help ensure consistent and reliable outputs. ### 4T Prompt Framework: - **Traits**: Define desired qualities (e.g., precise, honest). - **Task**: Specify what the model should do (e.g., answer using provided context). - **Tone**: Set communication style (e.g., educational, concise). - **Target**: Identify audience (e.g., computer science students). Example: > Traits: Be precise and source-grounded > Task: Answer using only the provided course material > Tone: Clear and educational > Target: A student studying LLM applications Using such structured prompts improves accuracy and relevance of generated content. ## Evaluation Metrics for LLMs Measuring LLM performance involves both automated metrics and qualitative assessments. ### Common Metrics: - **BLEU Score**: Measures overlap between generated and reference texts. - **ROUGE Score**: Evaluates summarization quality against references. - **Perplexity**: Assesses how well a model predicts sample text; lower is better. - **Human Evaluation**: Judges relevance, coherence, and factual correctness. Balancing quantitative scores with user feedback ensures practical usability of LLM systems.

Software Development life cycles: Waterfall Model lets make the waterfall model work for testing? resulted in the the V-model what about the iterative/Inceremental models? Interative - you create a little abit of the whole system, and approve upon it over each cycle Incremental - deliver er "fully" completet part of the system upon each completet cycle.   You can use the V-model on each user story. **Verifikation:** 1. **Unit Testing:** **testing of a single unit** 1. **  -** Usually a function or method.   - Sometimes called component testing   - it allows for regression test automation   - LOOK AT THE INTRODUCTION TO UNIT TESTING SLIDES for more information about different libraries Instead of Preconditions - Execution - Evaluation / Given When then Unit tests must be implemented for the business logic of the application Each unit test should only check one thing:   - what might be open for discussion   - Avoid more than one assertion per unit test   - Avoid branching within a unit test (ex using if) When a unit test fails, the code must be fixed immediately if all unit tests pass but the code crashes, a unit test that demonstrates the failure must be written The goal of unit testing is the enable the sustainable growth of the software project. The Advantages of Unit Testing:   - errors are found early   - **2. Integration Testing**   - Testing of parts the product involving different units **3. System Testing**   - simulation of real operation conditions in a live environment   - Full user paths might be tested instead of:   - Isolated units   - Integration between units   - here we tests what happens the system crashes   - Types of requirements tested:   - Functional requirements   - Functionalities the product must perform   - possible security requirements   - Interoperability constraints   - Non-functional requirements   - Stress testing   - Performance   - **Validation:** **4. Acceptance Testing**   - it demonstrates the systems conforms to:   - Customer requirements   - Operational processes   - Maintenance processes   - Usually based on the requirements specification (given by the customeer / PO   - Independent from other types of testing   - Mostly executed by the end users (could be PO in a scrum setting) Maintenance Testing: Testing changes on a deployed, stable product   - errors found in production   - Addition of unplanned features   - Migrations   - System retirement Demands full regression testing May involve parallel operations (old running til new is approved)

Static techniques test software without executing it They may find errors early in the life of the project They do not find failures, but causes of failures Static techniques: Review. Checking requirements and specification documents for errors before they are used in development. Usually manual Static Code Analysis. Analyse code for structural defects or systematic programming weaknesses. • Usually automatic • Performed by linters and most IDEs Reviews: systematic examination of a document to find and remove errors. Objectives: • Finding defects • Gaining understanding • Generating discussion • Decision making by consensus Objects: • Requirement specifications • System designs • Code • Test plans • Test cases Reviews and the V-model: Every step can be reviewed. A review is one of the earlies forms testing possible in a software development project (maybe beaten by risk analyst) Two major types: Code review: can be performed one to one Document review Basic process: • The document is studied by the reviewers • The author is informed of the issues found • The author updates the document accordingly • A next iteration is performed, if necessary Informal review • Documentation not required • Sometimes just a quick non-technical check • Inexpensive, but limited benefit Walkthrough • Review meeting led by the author of the document • It helps team members understand the document • Open-ended • Preparation and production of a report are optional Technical review (including code review) • Documented, well-defined detection process • Peer review, desirably with no presence of management • Preparation and report Inspection (most formal) • Led by a trained moderator • Peer examination of a document • Pre-meeting preparation • Production of an inspection report • Formal follow-up Formal review process: • Planning • Defining the purpose, scope, and time estimation of the review • Selecting staff and allocating roles • Defining the entry and exit criteria • Checking that the entry criteria is met • Initiation • Distributing the work product(s) to review • Explaining to the participants the objectives, scope, work product and roles of the review • Individual review • Reviewing the work product • Logging potential defects, questions and comments • Central phase (where most errors are caught) • Yet optional in low-formality reviews • Communication and analysis • Communicating potential defects in a meeting, log, emails, etc. • Analysing the defects • Evaluating the review results against the exit criteria • The work product can be fixed, or completely rejected Review roles and responsibilities: Manager • Decides to review and what to review • Designates staff and sets budget and timeframe • Not a part of the remaining review process Author • Author of the work product to review Moderator = review leader • Plans the review, follows up, mediates Reviewers Scribe = recorder • Documents issues summary review report: • Written by the moderator • Contains • Date of the meeting/communication period • Names and roles of the participants • Duration • Size of the work product • E.g., pages

review leader • Plans the review, follows up, mediates Reviewers Scribe = recorder • Documents issues summary review report: • Written by the moderator • Contains • Date of the meeting/communication period • Names and roles of the participants • Duration • Size of the work product • E.g., pages of an SRS, lines of code of a sw product • Defects found broken down by severity • Usually in tabular form, sorted and colour coded Success factors for reviews: • Objectives must be clearly predefined and agreed • Emphasis on learning and process improvement • Defects are welcomed • Defects must be expressed objectively • It is the documents, not the participants, that are reviewed • Techniques must also be reviewed • Checklists or similar must be used • Possible metrics to collect: • Time taken to review/inspect • Number of defects found • Percentage of project budget used/saved læ

Static Code Analysis Unit Testing: Basics, Best practices and anti-patterns, Approaches. TDD. Integration testing, Continuous testing. API testing. Database Testing. web performance testing - Stress performance testing. end-to-end UI testing - playright / cypress Usability testing Acceptance testing - Agile and testing. Materials: All will be provided.

white box testing is based on the structure of the code, your knowledge of the code, or a tools knowlegde of the code. the ideer of white box testing is reaching code( like hitting all "end points") Test coverage: techniques: statement and decison coverage some love it, other hate it. its an assesment technique and therefor for ppl outside coding likes the objective metherd of coverage. but! 100 coverage is really difficult/complecated. making enough test can be really time cunsuming, and alot of the tests could be unnessesary. a number of 75% coverage if good! but what if the remaining 25% is the importen parts of the application? testing trivial code does not give value.. but it does give coverage. Statement Coevreage: its basecly a focus on covering all executable code statements. measured: percentage of executale lines of code tested. the numbers of returns in the methord is the minimum amount of tests cases you need. If else satements also increases the amount of test cases only focusing on the coverage, you could end up writing bad tests just for the coverage. (please write a simple examples with inline explanations of stement coverage) in alot of situations its not a reliable measure, and for the most part a "political tool" for showing the bosses we are doing something. Decision Coverage also called branch coverage: every branch has to be tested atleast once. 100% decision coverage = 100% statements coverage, but not the otherway around. a lot better than just coverage. refactoring should not influence decison coverage. key is to find the none existing else'ses. (please write simple examples with inline explanations about Decision Coverage) write your tests with both black and white box techniques in mind. We should always use all ours tools at our dispotial to write good tests. box -> white -> intuition -> you end up with good and valid tests. general rule, usually 1 test is enough for a loop. big fan of data provider, we should use them as often as posible Experience-based techniques: used when: specifications is not adequate this is no time to run full structured tests: leave the company xD there are experienced professionals in the team as an addition to structured tests Techniques: Error guessing fault attack = software attack often used in security Exploratory testing session based exploratory testing. Time-limited Checklisting-based testing Static testing: Static code analysis is when we dont execute the code. dynamic testing is running the code. static testing helps find errors early in the life of the project, like a missing ; its finds causes of failures not actual failures. techniques: Review and static code analysis static code analysis is for finding syntax errors, enforce coding standards, test coverage. usually done with tools if can and should run the CI pipeline. typicaly helps finding: variables not being used, reference to variable with undefinded value, dead code, use of deprecated functionalities, violations of standeards, protential security issues. find so called "code smells", where your code looks sloppy.

standards, test coverage. usually done with tools if can and should run the CI pipeline. typicaly helps finding: variables not being used, reference to variable with undefinded value, dead code, use of deprecated functionalities, violations of standeards, protential security issues. find so called "code smells", where your code looks sloppy. really usefull for weakly typed languges.
